customer_anxiety_profile: William is a burned customer — panels on his roof, $32K loan from a Texas lender, Freedom Forever filing bankruptcy, waiting on BGE utility inspection. His primary anxiety is financial exposure and distrust of solar companies generally. He is NOT hostile to Ion; he ended the call warm and asking to stay on the list. His guard is up on cost claims ('no upfront cost' triggered immediate skepticism) but he responded well to the legitimacy credential and the empathy close.
unresolved_concerns: How to exit the Freedom Forever contract — he mentioned getting a lawyer tonight
Whether the $32K loan from the Texas lender is tied to the panels or separable
What happens to the panels already on his roof if he exits the contract
Whether Ion can work with him after contract exit given panels are already installed
sensitive_topics_raised: Freedom Forever bankruptcy — active legal dispute, lawyer engagement tonight
$32K loan from Texas lender — William believes he was overcharged
Panels already physically installed on roof — unusual situation for a new lead
BGE utility inspection pending — system not yet connected
exact_promises_rep_made: I'll keep you down. Notes in, and I'll keep you in.
I'll make sure you don't get bothered either.
closer_should_know: William is not a standard inbound lead — he has panels on his roof from a bankrupt competitor, an active $32K loan, and a lawyer meeting tonight. He is warm on Ion but will be hypersensitive to any cost framing that sounds like the Freedom Forever pitch. Do NOT lead with 'no upfront cost' — that triggered skepticism on this call. Lead with the situation read: acknowledge what he's dealing with, establish what Ion can and cannot do while he's in contract, and set clear expectations for the re-engagement timeline (~4 weeks). The rep promised he won't be bothered — honor that by making the callback feel like a follow-through on a relationship, not a new sales call.
